channel.title: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium
channel.description: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium

image.title: Stories by Bashkim Isai on Medium

item.title: [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

If you have been following along with this tutorial, you should have:

  • Identified which key (primary or secondary) is to be updated via $(activeKey) and $(cycleKey).
  • Updated the cycle key for each of your services.
  • Update each of your access tokens, connection strings, secrets, etc.

Once you have determine the keys that you would like to cycle, you are now ready to:

  • Update your active key — because your application should now be dependant on your newly cycled key.
  • Propagate your keys to your application (e.g.: via kubernetes).

Updating your active key

So that you can keep track of which key your application is using, you will need to update your active key secret in key vault.

parameters:
  - name: azureSubscription
    type: string

  # The name of the key vault where the active key is being stored
  - name: activeKeyVaultName
    type: string
  
  # The name of the secret in the key vault (above) of the active key
  - name: activeKeySecretName
    type: string
    default: active-key

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: "set active key"
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
      scriptType: ps
      scriptLocation: inlineScript
      inlineScript: |
        az keyvault secret set `
          --output none `
          --vault-name "${{ parameters.activeKeyVaultName }}" `
          --name "${{ parameters.activeKeySecretName }}" `
          --value "$(cycleKey)" `
          --content-type "string"

Propagating to your application

The remainder of this article will focus on how to propagate your key changes to an application running on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).

Consuming Key Vault secrets from Kubernetes

I have written another four-part article which describes the process of Injecting environment variables from App Configuration and Key Vault into AKS containers. Part three specifically discusses Azure Key Vault.

Using this process, you can remove the fetching of secrets out of your application — keeping your environment configuration separate from your application code.

Automatically deploying updated keys

Consider using Reloader, it’s a handy controller that watches your Secrets (from Azure Key Vault) and ConfigMaps (from Azure App Configuration). When a secret changes (say, from a key rotation) it automatically triggers a rolling restart of any deployments that it is consuming. With this setup:

  • Your pipeline updates the secret in Azure Key Vault.
  • Reloader picks up the change by polling Azure Key Vault periodically (e.g. every minute).
  • Reloader restarts your deployment automatically and uses the new key(s).

It’s a slick way to avoid manual intervention while keeping your infrastructure secure and up-to-date.

Firstly, install Reloader via helm charts using the instructions provided in GitHub. You can then configure each of your Kubernetes deployments to each of the Kubernetes secrets (and optionally, config maps) that contain your application secrets.

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: # DeploymentName
  annotations:
    configmap.reloader.stakater.com/reload: KubernetesConfigMapName
    secret.reloader.stakater.com/reload: KubernetesSecretName

spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: # DeploymentName
# ... etc

Final steps

You should now have all the tools required to cycle keys in your Azure services automatically via an Azure DevOps pipeline and propagate the changes to your Kubernetes services.

While there is no standard recommendation for the frequency that you rotate your keys, many providers recommend you update your keys every 90 days. With the system, you can cycle your keys every day, week, month, whatever you require.

item.title: [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime.
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

If you have been following this article, you should now have two pipeline variables that you can use to help you cycle your keys:

  • The $(activeKey) which identifies which key is currently in use.
  • The $(cycleKey) which is the key to be updated.

Both should have the value of either “primary” or “secondary”.

Now comes the fun part — each Azure service cycles keys in a different way, and while there are usually only two keys not all services have the same identifier for the primary and secondary key.

Structuring the cycling of your keys

For each Azure service, you will need to create a structure similar to the following:

  • Identify the way in which primary and secondary keys are used by the specific Azure Service via the documentation.
  • Cycle (or regenerate) only the $(cycleKey).
  • Save the updated key to the key vault for use by your Kubernetes services.

Cycling keys for Azure Services

This article will give you some examples of how to cycle keys for a limited number of Azure services, you will need to read the Azure documentation for a specific service to identify how those keys are cycled.

Azure Cache for Redis

Remember when I said that each service has their own identifiers for primary and secondary keys? In Azure Cache for Redis, this is Primary and Secondary — but in the output it’s named primaryKey and secondaryKey.

Read the documentation for Regenerating Keys for Azure Cache for Redis and then check out this practical example:

---
parameters:
  # enum of either "primary" or "secondary"
  - name: cycleKey
    type: string

  - name: azureSubscription
    type: string

  - name: resourceGroup
    type: string

  # the name of the redis service you wish to cycle keys for
  - name: redisName
    type: string

  # the key vault which contains the secrets
  - name: keyVaultName
    type: string

  # an array of secrets that should contain the most up-to-date key
  - name: keyVaultSecrets
    type: object # string[]

steps:
  - task: AzureCLI@2
    displayName: "redis: ${{ parameters.redisName }}"
    inputs:
      azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
      scriptType: ps
      scriptLocation: inlineScript
      inlineScript: |
        $CYCLE_NAME = If ("$(CycleKey)" -eq "primary") { 'Primary' } Else { 'Secondary' }
        $CYCLE_INDEX = If ("$(CycleKey)" -eq "primary") { 'primaryKey' } Else { 'secondaryKey' }
        $CYCLE_VALUE = $(az redis regenerate-keys `
          --output tsv --query $CYCLE_INDEX `
          --resource-group "${{ parameters.resourceGroup }}" `
          --name "${{ parameters.redisName }}" `
          --key-type ${CYCLE_NAME}
        )
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=cycleValue;issecret=true]${CYCLE_VALUE}"

  - ${{ each secret in parameters.keyVaultSecrets  }}:
      - task: AzureCLI@2
        displayName: "↳ secret: ${{ secret }}"
        inputs:
          azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
          scriptType: ps
          scriptLocation: inlineScript
          inlineScript: |
            az keyvault secret set `
              --vault-name "${{ parameters.keyVaultName }}" `
              --name "${{ secret }}" `
              --value "$(cycleValue)" `
              --content-type "string"

Breaking down each task:

  • It maps primary and secondary to the argument of key type used to identify which key is requested to be regenerated — either Primary or Secondary.
  • It maps primary and secondary to the response of the Azure CLI request to get the primaryKey or secondaryKey representing the changed value.
  • It regenerates the requested key and stores the value in a pipeline variable named $(cycleValue).
  • The last task loops through all the specified secrets where the keys are being used and stores the new key in those key vault secrets.

Other Azure services

There are so many Azure services which has keys and connection strings — writing code for each of them would be unreasonable, so here are some links to the Azure CLI commands you will need for some commonly used services.

  • Cognitive Services (OpenAI)
    az cognitiveservices account keys regenerate
    takes Key1 and Key2
    returns key1 and key2.
  • Cosmos DB
    az cosmosdb keys regenerate
    takes primary, secondary, primaryReadonly and secondaryReadonly
    returns primaryMasterKey, secondaryMasterKey, primaryReadonlyMasterKey, secondaryReadonlyMasterKey.
  • EventHub
    az eventhubs namespace authorization-rule keys renew
    takes PrimaryKey and SecondaryKey
    returns primaryConnectionString and secondaryConnectionString.
  • IoT Hub
    az iot hub policy renew-key
    takes primary and secondary
    returns primaryKey and secondaryKey.
  • Storage accounts
    az storage account keys renew
    takes key1 and key2
    returns an array with indexed values for the output.

As you can see, there is little consistency between each of the services. You will need to check the documentation to find the appropriate commands and properties.

What about passwords?

Services like PostgreSQL don’t use keys for access control, instead you access the service using a password.

Hopefully you’re not using the superuser in your environments and you’ll have a separate user for connecting to your database. But instead of having just one user, you’ll want to manage two separate user accounts (e.g.: a primary and secondary user), then you’ll need to:

  • Generate a new password in the pipeline.
  • Connect to the PostreSQL service.
  • Update the password with an SQL statement.

Now that you know how to cycle your keys, you can continue to read how to update your active key and propagate the keys to your application.

Part 4 of 4: Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

item.title: [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime.
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

Prerequisites

While you can follow along with the article for a theoretical knowledge of cycling keys, the following article assumes you have:

  • Access to an Azure subscription.
  • A basic understanding of Azure services and which services require a key in order to be cycled.
  • Azure DevOps set up with a service connection for your Azure Subscription.

Determining which key is active

Before cycling keys, you need to know which key your application is using. — this is called your active key. If your application is currently using your active key and you cycle it, you will experience downtime.

If you’re storing keys in Azure Key Vault, that’s a great starting point and there are many ways that you can do this, including:

  • Storing a secret in Azure Key Vault (e.g.: called “active-key”) which identifies which key services should be currently using.
  • Use Azure CLI or REST API commands in your pipeline to query the current key and match it with what’s stored in Key Vault.

This example will take the first approach of using an active key in Azure Key Vault to identify which key is currently in use. It’s a little simpler and means that all your services will be using the same key reference.

# pipelines/steps/active-cycle-keys.yml

parameters:
  # The name of your service connection for your azure subscription
  - name: azureSubscription
    type: string

  # The name of the key vault where the active key is being stored
  - name: activeKeyVaultName
    type: string
  
  # The name of the secret in the key vault (above) of the active key
  - name: activeKeySecretName
    type: string
    default: active-key

steps: 
  - task: AzureCLI@2
     displayName: "get the active key"
     inputs:
       azureSubscription: ${{ parameters.azureSubscription }}
       scriptType: ps
       scriptLocation: inlineScript
       inlineScript: |
         $_ACTIVE_KEY = $(az keyvault secret show --name "${{ parameters.activeKeySecretName }}" --vault-name "${{ parameters.activeKeyVaultName }}" --query value --output tsv)
         $ACTIVE_KEY = If ("${_ACTIVE_KEY}" -eq "secondary") { 'secondary' } Else { 'primary' }
         echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=activeKey;]${ACTIVE_KEY}"

  # Debugging in the pipeline
  - task: Bash@3
    displayName: "cycle the primary key"
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['activeKey'], 'secondary'))
    inputs:
      targetType: "inline"
      script: |
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=cycleKey;]primary"

  # Debugging in the pipeline
  - task: Bash@3
    displayName: "cycle the secondary key"
    condition: and(succeeded(), eq(variables['activeKey'], 'primary'))
    inputs:
      targetType: "inline"
      script: |
        echo "##vso[task.setvariable variable=cycleKey;]secondary"

This set of steps consists of three tasks:

  • The first task identifies what is the current active-key in the key vault and stores it in a pipeline variable called $(activeKey).
  • The second and third tasks set a pipeline variable called $(cycleKey) to either primary or secondary. Splitting it in this way visually informs you during pipeline execution as to which key is being rotated.

Now that you have identified your active and cycle keys, continue reading:

Part 3 of 4: Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.

item.title: [1/4] Cycling keys, access tokens and connection strings in your CI/CD pipelines with zero downtime
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on the concept of cycling keys with a practical implementation with Azure DevOps pipelines and Azure services — but you can apply the same theory to any cloud provider.

  • [1/4] Cycling keys in your CI pipelines on Azure DevOps and Azure with zero downtime.
  • [2/4] Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.
  • [3/4] Cycling keys in Azure services with Azure DevOps.
  • [4/4] Automatically propagating keys to Kubernetes services.

If you’ve ever worked with secrets, connection strings or access tokens, you probably know how easy it is to forget they’re even there until something breaks. Keys require maintenance for security and maintainability and a big part of doing it right is knowing when (and how) to cycle your keys.

This article is for developers and DevOps engineers who want a hands-on, practical guide to implementing key rotation within their Azure DevOps pipelines, specifically when working with Azure resources. It discusses what key cycling involves, how to identify and update keys in Azure, and how to automate the entire process using pipelines — and even take it a step further with Kubernetes integration.

While this article focuses on Azure DevOps for pipelines and Azure Services to cycle keys, it also describes the principles for cycling keys allowing you to use it in any cloud platform provider (AWS, GCP) and any CI/CD pipeline tool (CircleCi, GitHub Actions, etc.)

Why is it important to cycle keys?

Key rotation reduces your attack surface. The longer a key stays in use, the more time there is for it to be leaked, stolen, or suffer a brute force attack. Regularly rotating your keys limits the damage in the event of a compromise, ensures compliance with security policies, and enforces good operational hygiene across your team and infrastructure.

Implementing the cycling of keys in an automated processes also reduces the risk for human error — you’ll never forget to copy/paste the keys to a service you forgot about, it frees up time to focus on more important things, you’ll never forget to do it and a human will never need to know what the keys are as they will be cycled automatically.

The process of cycling keys

In Azure, many services offer primary and secondary keys. This dual-key approach allows you to rotate keys without downtime. Here’s how the process typically works:

  1. Determine the current (active) key in use.
  2. Rotate the unused (cycle) key.
  3. Update your applications and secrets to use the newly rotated cycle key.
  4. Validate that your new key is in use.
  5. Repeat the process with the now-unused key.

This approach ensures that at least one valid key is always available, so services depending on the key can continue running while you handle the update.

Now that you’re familiar with the concept of cycling keys, how it works and why it’s important, continue reading:

Part 2 of 4: Identifying your active and cycle keys in Azure.

item.title: Integrating react-hook-form with Material UI and Zod
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
Integrating react hook form with Material UI and Zod

This article describes the principles implemented in the react hook form integrations storybook — check it out before continuing to read.

Combining Material UI with react hook form may appear straightforward at first glance. However, in practice, the integration—particularly when working with controlled components—can introduce unexpected complexity.

This article describes some common pitfalls, how to avoid them and provides some resources to help effectively implement these libraries.

Article structure

This article explains how to integrate component libraries with react hook form; but you can instead work through the storybook implementation for practical implementations of multiple scenarios.

To get the most out of this article, the recommended approach is to:

  • Identify the component library and target component in the storybook implementation.
  • Review the GitHub source for the corresponding component to understand how it has been implemented.
  • Refer to the explanations below for context on known pitfalls and workarounds.

Repository file structure

Various validation libraries are compatible with @hookform/resolvers (including Yup and Joi), but all examples provided use Zod.

Each example in the react hook forms GitHub repository typically consists of:

  • schema.ts — defines an example Zod schema for the form field, often with a “required” constraint.
  • view.tsx — contains the integration logic between the component (e.g., from Material UI) and react hook form, including error handling.
  • stories.tsx — demonstrates the component within a form rendered in Storybook.

Required dependencies

The following packages are required to implement the patterns described:

  • React Hook Form — Handles form state and validation integration.
  • A component library — for example: Material UI.
  • A schema validation tool — for example: Zod, Yup or Joi.

Additional utilities from react hook form:

  • @hookform/error-message — displays user-friendly validation errors.
  • @hookform/resolvers — connects schema validation tools to react hook form.

The remainder of this article describes some of the common pitfalls and how to avoid them.

Controlled vs. uncontrolled state

If you’re not aware of the difference between controlled and uncontrolled state, read the React documentation regarding controlled components. But in a nutshell:

  • Controlled components use React state for form data, enabling real-time updates and validation.
  • Uncontrolled components delegate data handling to the DOM via refs, which can simplify simple forms but reduce flexibility.

The simplest way to define a component as having controlled state is to define its value property; however, a problem occurs when the default value of a form field is undefined.

Take the following (simplified) example:

import TextField from "@mui/material/TextField";
import { Controller, useForm } from "react-hook-form";

export default function MyTextField() {
  const form = useForm();

  return (
    <Controller
      name="myTextField"
      control={form.control}
      render={({ field, fieldState }) => (
        <TextField {...field} label="My Text field" />
      )}
    />
  );
}

In this case, the absence of a default value causes the TextField to transition from an uncontrolled to a controlled state, triggering a React warning.

Define default values in useForm (not recommended)

Setting a default value for the form field as a string so that it will never be undefined:

const form = useForm({
  defaultValues: {
    myTextField: ""
  },
});

While this method prevents the uncontrolled-to-controlled transition, it does not scale well for forms that require reusability — such as those used in CRUD operations. You can still use defaultValues when you want to set default values for your form fields (e.g.: when you’re editing an entity).

Default value at the component level (recommended)

Instead of modifying the form’s initial state, apply a fallback value directly in the field rendering logic.

<Controller
  name="myTextField"
  control={form.control}
  render={({ field: { value = "", ...field }, fieldState }) => (
    <TextField {...field} value={value} label="My Text field" />
  )}
/>

This ensures the component renders with an empty string when the value is undefined, while preserving the underlying form state.

Handling onChange for non-standard components

Certain components, such as Material UI’s Autocomplete component, use a non-standard onChange signature, which is incompatible with react hook form by default.

Autocomplete's onChange function has the following signature:

(event: React.SyntheticEvent, value: T | T[], reason: string) => void

By contrast, react hook form expects a single argument to be passed to its onChange. Here is an initial implementation that will not function correctly:

import Autocomplete from "@mui/material/Autocomplete";
import TextField from "@mui/material/TextField";
import { Controller, useForm } from "react-hook-form";

const seasons = ["Summer", "Autumn", "Winter", "Spring"];

export default function MyAutocomplete() {
  const form = useForm();

  return (
    <Controller
      name="myAutocomplete"
      control={form.control}
      render={({ field: { value = "", ...field }, fieldState }) => (
        <Autocomplete
          {...field}
          options={seasons}
          value={value}
          renderInput={(params) => <TextField {...params} label="Season" />}
        />
      )}
    />
  );
}

To correct this, destructure the onChange handler and modify its behavior to extract only the relevant value:


<Controller
  name="myAutocomplete"
  control={form.control}
  /* 👇 Destructure onChange out of the field */
  render={({ field: { onChange, value = "", ...field }, fieldState }) => (
    <Autocomplete
      {...field}
      options={seasons}
      value={value}
      /* 👇 Pass the data argument as the first onChange argument */
      onChange={(_e, data) => onChange(data)}
      renderInput={(params) => <TextField {...params} label="Season" />}
    />
  )}
/>

This approach ensures that onChange receives only the data value, allowing the form to update correctly.

Final thoughts

With a few targeted adjustments, most component libraries can be successfully integrated with react hook form, allowing for robust client-side validation and seamless form experiences in modern React applications.

item.title: Enhance your Strapi content with rich media using oEmbed
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
Embedding content like YouTube videos, tweets, or Instagram posts is a powerful way to make your content more engaging. But for many non-technical users, dealing with iframe codes or embed scripts can be confusing — and even risky for security and content consistency.

That’s where oEmbed comes in.

Why oEmbed?

oEmbed is a standard format to retrieve metadata for assets hosted on external websites by simply pasting a URL. The platform (like YouTube or Twitter) sends back a structured response which can include the title, thumbnail, embed HTML, etc. — so editors don’t need to touch any code.

With oEmbed, you get:

✅ Ease for Non-Developers: Editors just paste a URL. No need to mess with iframes or copy-pasting embed code from third-party platforms.

✅ Performance & Security: Only essential metadata is stored — no uploading media, and no risky script handling. It keeps your website lightweight and secure.

✅ Consistent UX: With structured data, you can control how embedded content appears across your site, so every video, tweet, or post looks uniform and on-brand.

✅ Cost Effective: Storing large files like videos on a third-party can decrease your server load and costs.

There are (literally) hundreds of websites available online where you can take advantage of oEmbed — the oEmbed website publishes a list of oEmbed providers. Some of the most popular websites include:

  • Video: YouTube, Vimeo, TikTok, Twitch, DailyMotion
  • Photo: Imgur, Flickr, DeviantArt, Unsplash, Instagram
  • Rich: Twitter, Pinterest, SlideShare, SoundCloud, CodePen, JSFiddle

Want to drop in a Tweet? a SoundCloud track? or a Pinterest board? It works exactly the same way — just paste the link.

Integrating with Strapi v5

If you’re using Strapi v5, check out strapi-plugin-oembed. It allows editors to paste an oEmbed-supported URL and get a nice visual representation in the admin.

Key features

  • Designed for Strapi v5 using the custom fields API.
  • Beautiful, native-feeling preview UI for non-technical authors.
  • Returns the full oEmbed response in your API (URL, HTML, metadata).
  • No extra API calls or rendering logic is needed on the backend.

Installation

Add the dependency to your project:

# npm
npm install --save strapi-plugin-oembed

# yarn
yarn add strapi-plugin-oembed

Enable the plugin in your config/plugins.ts:

/* ./config/plugins.ts */

export default () => ({
  // ...
  oembed: {
    enabled: true,
  },
});

And add the new field to your existing content-type model:

/* ./src/api/[content-type]/content-types/[content-type]/schema.json */

{
  "kind": "collectionType",
  "collectionName": "[content-type]",
  "attributes": {
    // 👇 these lines ...
    "fieldName": {
      "type": "customField",
      "customField": "plugin::oembed.oembed"
    }
    // ...
  }
}

Front-end example

Let’s say your front-end (e.g., Next.js or Gatsby) receives an oEmbed response from your Strapi API like this:

{
  "url": "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aqz-KE-bpKQ",
  "thumbnail": "data:image/jpeg;base64,...truncated...",
  "oembed": {
    "title": "Big Buck Bunny 60fps 4K - Official Blender Foundation Short Film",
    "author_name": "Blender",
    "author_url": "https://www.youtube.com/@BlenderOfficial",
    "type": "video",
    "height": 113,
    "width": 200,
    "version": "1.0",
    "provider_name": "YouTube",
    "provider_url": "https://www.youtube.com/",
    "thumbnail_height": 360,
    "thumbnail_width": 480,
    "thumbnail_url": "https://i.ytimg.com/vi/aqz-KE-bpKQ/hqdefault.jpg",
    "html": "<iframe width=\"200\" height=\"113\" src=\"https://www.youtube.com/embed/aqz-KE-bpKQ?feature=oembed\" frameborder=\"0\" allow=\"accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share\" referrerpolicy=\"strict-origin-when-cross-origin\" allowfullscreen title=\"Big Buck Bunny 60fps 4K - Official Blender Foundation Short Film\"></iframe>"
  }
}

You have a few choices:

  • The oembed.html field in this example includes an iframe you can drop directly into the page using dangerouslySetInnerHTML, but rendering raw HTML like this can introduce security risks — especially if you’re handling user-submitted content.
  • Alternatively, you could use some of the metadata to display vital information, and then use react-player to render the video player:
import React from 'react';
import ReactPlayer from 'react-player';

export default function VideoPlayer({ video }) => {
  const { url, oembed } = video;

  if (!ReactPlayer.canPlay(url)) {
    return null;
  }

  return (
    <figure>
      <ReactPlayer url={oembed.url} controls width="100%" />
      <figcaption>
        <h3>{oembed.title}</h3>
        <p>By: {oembed.author_name}</p>
      </figcaption>
    </figure>
  );
};

This ensures your embeds are safe, responsive, and beautifully rendered — no messy HTML needed. Additionally, the admin stays visual and doesn’t require any complex code from the editors.

Final thoughts

Whether you’re building a blog, a knowledge base, or a custom CMS, rich media can elevate your content — and oEmbed can make that process easy:

  • Editors stay focused on content, not code.
  • Your app stays performant and secure.
  • Users enjoy consistent, high-quality embeds.

strapi-plugin-oembed on: Strapi Market, Npm and GitHub

item.title: Validating file uploads and their contents with Zod in TypeScript
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
Zod provides out-of-the-box functionality to parse data against a schema for basic primitive and reference types; however, some scenarios require more advanced practices — such as ensuring a file’s contents are valid.

When used together, these three core concepts allow you to manage file uploads and verify that their contents are sane:

  • z.custom() — to create a schema for an object.
  • .transform() — to read the file’s contents.
  • .superRefine() — to validate the contents in a custom manner.

Validating file uploads

If you’re not interested in file uploads and would only like to validate the file’s contents, you can skip to the next section.

When handling a list of files from a form submission, the browser provides a FileList object regardless of whether you allow single or multiple files to be uploaded from an input. In essence, Zod can handle a FileList as an immutable array of File objects.

import { z } from "zod";

/* fileList as FileList */

const fileListSchema = z.array( ... );
fileListSchema.parse(fileList);

While Zod doesn’t have a specific schema, we can use z.custom<File>() to handle these objects.

import { z } from "zod";

const fileSchema = z.custom<File>();
const fileListSchema = z.array(fileSchema);
fileListSchema.parse(fileList);

Maybe you want there to be exactly one file to upload?

z.array(z.custom<File>()).length(1);

And if you’re going to be using this in a form, you can wrap this part of an object schema:

import { z } from "zod";

const fileSchema = z.custom<File>();
const formSchema = z.object({
  reportFile: z.array(fileSchema).length(1)
});

Transforming the file contents

For this part of the tutorial, we’re going to be managing the single file. If you’re using file uploads, make sure you use the z.object() and z.array() described above.

Now that we’re sure that we have a file, we want to check the contents of the file to make sure that it is valid. Our case study will read a file and check that it is a parsable JSON file.

The structure

We’ll make this code configurable and reusable.

import { RefinementCtx, z } from "zod";

export type TransformFileToContentsResult = {
  file: File;
  contents: string;
};

function transformFileToContents() {
  return async function (
    file: File,
    ctx: RefinementCtx
  ): Promise<TransformFileToContentsResult> {
    try {
      const contents = await file.text();
      return { file, contents };
    } catch (err) {
      ctx.addIssue({
        code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
        message: "Unable to read file.",
      });
    }
  };
}

In our case study, the function transformFileToContents currently takes no arguments because we don’t need it to be configurable. However, your situation may require some options so that you can reuse the transformer.

/* Passing no options */
function transformFileToContents() { ... }

/* Passing options */
export type TransformFileToContentsOptions = {
  verbose: boolean;
};

function transformFileToContents(
  options: TransformFileToContentsOptions
) { ... }

It then returns an asynchronous function which will perform the transformation when the Zod validation is run.

return async function (
  file: File,
  ctx: RefinementCtx
): Promise<TransformFileToContentsResult> {
  ...
}

The returned arrow function receives two parameters from Zod:

  • The file argument contains a File object representing the file selected by the user.
  • The ctx (context) argument contains access to the refinement context where you can report errors if you’re unable to transform the file effectively. Although we are performing a transform and not a refinement, Zod uses the same type for both of these functions.

The function then returns TransformFileContentResult asynchronously with two properties:

  • The file property references the original File object that the user uploaded.
  • The contents property contains the file’s contents to be validated later.

The transformation

In our transformer, the try block contains code to read the file’s contents and returns a TransformFileContentResult if everything is successful. This allows schema validations to be added in the future.

const contents = await file.text();
return { file, contents }; /* TransformFileContentResult */

The catch block uses the refinement context passed through from Zod to attach an error (or issue) to the validation of that specific field. This example returns a simple error message, but you can extend this to add err.message if it doesn’t expose critical technical aspects.

try {
  ...
} catch (err) {
  ctx.addIssue({
    code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
    message: "Unable to read file.",
  });
}

Getting the Schema input and output types

You may notice that the schema changes shape when the transform is applied. Take the following example:

export type SchemaInput = {
  reportFile: Array<File>;
}

/* Transforms to */

export type SchemaOutput = {
  reportFile: Array<{
    file: File;
    contents: string;
  }>
}

As Zod is a TypeScript-first schema validation with static type inference, you may be familiar with z.infer<typeof schema>. But Zod also exposes z.input and z.output to infer the schema’s types.

import { z } from "zod";

const formSchema = z.object({
  reportFile: z.array(
    z.custom<File>()
      .transform(transformFileToContents())
  ).length(1)
});

const SchemaInputType = z.input<typeof formSchema>;
const SchemaOutputType = z.output<typeof formSchema>; /* z.output or z.infer */

Validating the file contents

We can validate the file’s contents with Zod. In our case study, we want to ensure that the uploaded file is a valid JSON file. We can use superRefine() to ensure that the browser can parse the string contents of the file as a JSON object.

import { z } from "zod";

const formSchema = z.object({
  reportFile: z.array(
    z.custom<File>()
      .transform(transformFileToContents())
      .superRefine(refineContentsIsJSON())
  ).length(1)
});

As the transform pattern is similar to the refinement pattern, we won’t go into depth about each line of code.

import { RefinementCtx, z } from "zod";
import { TransformFileToContentsResult } from "./transform";

function refineContentsIsJSON() {
  return async function (
    result: TransformFileContentResult,
    ctx: RefinementCtx
  ): Promise<TransformFileToContentsResult> {
    try {
      /* Throws an error if the JSON is invalid */
      JSON.parse(result.contents);
    } catch (err) {
      ctx.addIssue({
        code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
        message: "Unable to parse JSON.",
      });
    }
  };
}

Unit tests

Because the transform and the refinement functions are modular, they are also very easy to test without even mounting Zod.

import { transformFileToContents } from "./transform-file-to-contents";
import { refineContentsIsJSON } from "./refine-contents-is-json";

describe("transformFileToContents", () => {
  it("should transform and validate a valid JSON file", () => {
    const validFile = new File(["{\"valid\": true}"], "valid.json", {});
    const ctx = { addIssue: jest.fn(), path: [""] };

    const transform = transformFileToContents();
    const transformed = transform(validFile, ctx);
    expect(ctx.addIssue).not.toHaveBeenCalled();

    const refine = refineContentsIsJSON();
    refine(transformed, ctx);
    expect(ctx.addIssue).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
  });

  it("should add an issue with invalid json", () => {
    const invalidFile = new File(["lorem ipsum"], "invalid.json", {});
    const ctx = { addIssue: jest.fn(), path: [""] };

    const transform = transformFileToContents();
    const transformed = transform(invalidFile, ctx);
    expect(ctx.addIssue).not.toHaveBeenCalled();

    const refine = refineContentsIsJSON();
    refine(transformed, ctx);
    expect(ctx.addIssue).toHaveBeenCalled();
  });
});

What next?

This example has shown you how to structure the transforming and refinement in a Zod schema so that you can read the contents of files and validate that their contents are as expected — and there are plenty of ways to utilise this pattern. For example:

  • It doesn’t have to be just text or JSON files, with the right libraries you can also read and parse binary files (e.g.: PDF, etc.)
  • You can also ensure the contents are in the correct format. Are you validating a package.json ? Why not make sure that the fields have been entered correctly? You can do this with external libraries linking in through superRefine().

item.title: Using modals in Next.js with parallel routes (slots), route groups and interceptors
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
If you’ve ever been on Unsplash or Instagram’s desktop website, you may notice that depending on the context of your visiting you will receive a different user experience when enlarging a photo. Try it out now …

On your desktop browser, head over to Unsplash and search for your favourite topic (I’m doing SCUBA diving) — take note of the URL.

Clicking on a photo from the search results will display a dialog over the top of the page. You’ll notice that the URL will have changed to that of the individual picture:

  • Example search URL: https://unsplash.com/s/photos/scuba.
  • Example photo URL: https://unsplash.com/photos/a-person-in-a-scuba-suit-is-standing-on-a-diving-device-3IhLFF6gvCs.

If you refresh the page while the dialog is open, you might logically expect to receive the same experience — search results in the background and a dialog displaying the large format picture.

Instead, the website serves a page that’s fully dedicated to the image that you selected with no search results in the background.

Since the web page has no context about the search history it has to render the content specific to the URL, equating to two possible user experiences:

  • When the application has context for the user’s intention— the user experience is tailored to the user’s context. In this example, the user is searching for images and viewing the large format in a dialog while keeping the search results accessible but in the background.
  • When the application has no context for the user’s intention — the user experience is specific to the deep-linked URL.

There are a few benefits to this approach:

  • It’s better for SEO — each image page can be indexed by a search engine.
  • It’s easier to share — the asset is shareable through a deep link.
  • It’s easier to manage the state from the user’s intention — preserving the user’s context (their search results) in the background.
  • It’s a better user experience — the model closes when you go backwards in history (rather than going to the previous route) and reopens when going forward.

In Next.js, this process can be achieved using some of the functionality introduced in Next.js version 13. Before beginning this tutorial, read through the documentation on the Next.js website on the following topics:

  • Parallel routes — for injecting a modal into the page as a slot.
  • Intercepting routes — for hooking into the browser History API.
  • Dynamic routes — for showing individual photos.
  • Route groups — for isolating modals on specific pages, so they don’t conflict.
I recommend using at least Next 15, as some issues were found in version 14 when interlacing interceptors with dynamic routes.

Objective

Follow this article with the code repository on GitHub.

This tutorial will demonstrate a basic implementation of the Unsplash website using some of its images. The user will:

  • Land on a page which lists images from Unsplash.
  • Clicking a picture will open a dialog as a modal over the page to display the image in a larger format.
  • Refreshing the page or sharing the URL will show the standalone photo on the page without a dialog as a modal.

This example utilises Material UI to rapidly prototype the front end; however, you can transpose these concepts to your own design system.

To begin, have a look at the final code implementation on GitHub.

Understanding key files

Here’s a walkthrough of the most important files which exist in the application from the GitHub repository.

Simplified code snippets
We’ll go through each of the files, but the code samples are simplified for brevity. The full code is available in the repository on GitHub.

src/app/(home)/page.tsx — on GitHub

This page contains a list of all the photos in our photos array, with each one being linked to its individual page to view a larger format. It doesn’t matter if the URL is shown as a dialog or a separate page, Next.js will take care of that for us.

import Link from "next/link";

{photos.map((photo) => (
  <Link href={`/photos/${photo.id}`} key={photo.id}>
    <img {...photo} />
  </Link>
))}
Remember:
To make use of interceptors, you must use the Link component from Next.js instead of an <a> anchor tag.

After checking out the repository and running npm install and npm run dev, the page should appear as follows:

src/app/(home)/layout.tsx — on GitHub

Slots are injected into layouts and cannot be injected into a page. In our scenario, our slot is called modals.

import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export type HomeLayoutProps = Readonly<PropsWithChildren<{
  modals: ReactNode;
}>>;

export default async function HomeLayout({
  children,
  modals,
}: HomeLayoutProps) {
  return (
    <>
      {children}
      {modals}
    </>
  );
}

You’ll notice that these two files (page.tsx and layout.tsx) are encapsulated in a folder named (home).

We don’t want the modals to be available on any subpages, only on the home page which lists the URL. Any subpaths (such as /photos/[id]) should not be affected. In order to do this, we utilise route groups in Next.js to isolate the layout and slots, allowing for extra layouts that do not interfere with sub-directories. Route groups (surrounded by parenthesis) are not parsed from the URL.

src/app/(home)/@modals/default.tsx — on GitHub

When the layout first loads, Next.js doesn’t have a state for any slot because it hasn’t dynamically intercepted any routes yet. Instead, it will fall back to this default.tsx file sitting in the root of the slot directory.

Because we don’t want to display anything, we simply can return null.

export default function HomePageModalsSlot() {
  return null;
}

src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx — on GitHub

This is your intercepted route, where our dialog will appear. This page will contain modal-specific design elements (e.g.: close button) which should not appear on the deep link page.

When you navigate to an individual photo, instead of using the code in src/app/photos/[id]/page.tsx, Next.js will use this interceptor instead. Users will continue their experience on the current page (in our example, listing all the photos), but the modals slot will update to inject the dialog.

Remember:
When you create, edit, remove or change the file structure in your app directory (especially with new slots and interceptors) you need to restart Next.js to affect the changes. Individual changes to the file contents don’t usually need a manual restart.

src/app/photos/[id]/page.tsx — on GitHub

This page is rendered when you navigate to the photo URL directly via deep link, or if you refresh the photo while viewing it in a modal. It should be optimised for fast loading and SEO.

You’ll notice that it is not inside the (home) folder, this is because we do not want the modals to be available on this page. If you want to group this page into its own route group, you could also name the file as: src/app/(photo)/photos/[id]/page.tsx.

import { photos } from "@/libraries/photos";
import { notFound } from "next/navigation";

export type PhotoPageProps = Readonly<{
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}>;

export default async function PhotoPage({ params }: PhotoPageProps) {
  const { id } = await params;

  const photo = photos[id];
  if (!photo) {
    notFound();
  }

  return (
    <img {...photo} />
  )
}

While viewing the dialog, you can refresh the page to see the deep link. You’ll notice there is no Close button as the image is not being displayed in a dialog.

Closing the modal

There are two ways that the modal can be closed.

Browser back button

When the user presses the back button in the browser, Next.js will switch the modals slot back to the default.tsx handler. In our example, this just returns null so the modal disappears. Unfortunately, there’s no way that I’ve found at the moment to animate the modal closing if you press the back button, as Next.js doesn’t allow you to hook into browser events.

Calling router.back()

You can also create a close button in the modal. With dialogs in Material UI, the modal will also listen for the ESC key to trigger the onClose event.

When onClose is triggered, you will want to create a callback which hooks into the Next.js navigation router to go back in history.

"use client";

import { useRouter } from "next/navigation";

const router = useRouter();

<Dialog open onClose={router.back()}> ... </Dialog>

Advice for naming conventions

Interceptors

You must get the correct path for your interceptors. I had some issues because the path couldn’t be interpreted by Next.js.

  • ✅ src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
    This is the correct naming convention.
  • ❌ src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)/photos/[id]/page.tsx
    Although it might appear that this will work, the extra slash after the interceptor will cause issues.

Parallel routes (slots)

Because the name of your slot is used as a variable in your code, you want to avoid using hyphens in the slot’s file name.

  • ✅ src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
    Works because modals is a single word.
  • ✅ src/app/(home)/@photoModals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
    Also works because photoModals is in camelCase.
  • ✅ src/app/(home)/@photoModals/(.)photo-view/[id]/page.tsx
    You can still use hyphens in your path too.
  • ❌ src/app/(home)/@photo-modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
    This causes problems as photo-modals is not a good variable name in JavaScript/TypeScript.

It’s also worthwhile noting that you can add multiple interceptors in a single slot. This can be useful for keeping your folder structure clean.

src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/delete/page.tsx
src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/update/page.tsx
src/app/(home)/@modals/default.tsx

One or many slots?

Is it better to create a single slot or use multiple slots to set up your interceptors? There are scenarios for both.

It’s more efficient to use a single slot and provide multiple interceptors inside in the use case of modals unless you’re overlaying modals.

Consider the following paths:

src/app/(home)/@photoView/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx 
src/app/(home)/@photoCreate/(.)photos/create/page.tsx

There are two separate slots — photoView and photoCreate and both are rendered on the page at once. When navigating to the path /photos/create, both the photoView and photoCreate slots will be rendered — with the [id] being the value of create. This isn’t what we want, because we don’t have a photo with the ID of create — which should probably throw an error.

Instead, keep all the modals in one slot:

src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx
src/app/(home)/@modals/(.)photos/create/page.tsx

Here, the path after photos/ can either have an id or the word create. Using a single slot, only one of the two interceptors will appear at any time and because create is a literal value, it takes priority over [id], a dynamic route.

Conclusion

By utilising four pieces of functionality introduced in Next.js 13 (parallel routes, intercepting routes, dynamic routes and route groups), you can create a great user experience and SEO-friendly implementation of an application with modals.

There are plenty of different types of modals and components you can implement this approach with in Material UI — this tutorial has shown you how to implement dialog boxes, but you can also the same concept for drawers, menus, popovers and tabs to name a few — or try implementing it with your own design system.

item.title: [4/4] Configuring Azure Kubernetes Services containers to consume ConfigMap and Secret resources
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on how to configure App Configuration and Key Vault to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).

  • [1/4] Injecting environment variables from App Configuration and Key Vault into AKS containers
  • [2/4] Configuring App Configuration to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Services
  • [3/4] Configuring Key Vault to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Services
  • [4/4] Configuring Azure Kubernetes Services to consume ConfigMap and Secret resources

Azure Kubernetes Service

Now that you have set up, authenticated and configured the Azure App Configuration and/or Key Vault to communicate with the AKS cluster, the final step is to set up a container which will consume the ConfigMap and/or Secrets.

Configuration

Below is an example of a Kubernetes deployment to create a pod which attaches the necessary resources to bind the ConfigMaps and Secrets to the environment variables.

# web-deployment.yml

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  replicas: 1,
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: "https://registry.host/repo:tag"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          # important code below
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: web-appconfig
            - secretRef:
                name: web-keyvault
          volumeMounts:
            - name: web-appconfig-volume
              mountPath: /mnt/web-appconfig
              readOnly: true
            - name: web-keyvault-volume
              mountPath: /mnt/web-keyvault
              readOnly: true
      volumes:
        - name: web-appconfig-volume
          configMap:
            name: web-appconfig
        - name: web-keyvault-volume
          csi:
            driver: secrets-store.csi.k8s.io
            readOnly: true
            volumeAttributes:
              secretProviderClass: web-keyvault

There are three important parts to importing from the Kubernetes secrets (from Key Vault) and the Kubernetes ConfigMap (from App Configuration):

  • envFrom — the configuration of Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets where the container will pull the environment variables.
  • volumes — these are Kubernetes volumes which contain your secrets and ConfigMaps in individual files.
  • volumeMounts — describes how to mount the Kubernetes volumes so that the contents are accessible from the container.

N.B.: For the environment variables to be available in the application, Kubernetes needs to have the environment variables accessible in the container as a volume mount — so even if you’re not using the file system for your environment variables, these are still required.

Deployment

You can then deploy your Deployment resource using kubectl:

kubectl apply -f web-deployment.yml

If you’re using Azure DevOps, you can take advantage of the Kubernetes@1 task:

- task: Kubernetes@1
  inputs:
    connectionType: "Kubernetes Service Connection"
    kubernetesServiceEndpoint: "Kubernetes Service Connection Name"
    command: "apply"
    arguments: "-f web-deployment.yml"

Debugging

To check and see if the environment variables have been set properly, you need to get the name of your generated pod from the deployment and execute the env command to list all your environment variables

Get a list of your running pods:

$ kubectl get pod
NAME                                       READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
web-fc8e2a809-cs22t                        1/1     Running   0          3m

Note down the name of the generated pod. In this case, its name is web-fc8e2a809-cs22t. You can then use it to get a list of all environment variables set in your environment.

kubectl exec --stdin --tty "web-fc8e2a809-cs22t" -- /bin/env | sort

You will see a long list of environment variables that have been set in your environment — some of them will be system-defined, others will be injected by Kubernetes and finally some will be from your ConfigMaps and Secrets. Simply check the sorted list to ensure that the variables you expect are available.

You can also connect to a container and check the mounted volumes:

# Connect using a BASH shell
kubectl exec --stdin --tty web-fc8e2a809-cs22t -- /bin/bash

# Once connected, you can list all environment variables and sort them
env | sort

# Alternatively, you can check the /mnt directory to see which 
# ConfigMaps and Secrets have been mounted
ls -l /mnt

Utilisation

You now should have your variables defined in your container and they should be ready to use in your application. You can access them like any other environment variable, for example in JavaScript:

process.env["APP_ENV"];

item.title: [3/4] Configuring Key Vault to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Services
item.dc:creator: Bashkim Isai
item.content:encoded:
This is a multi-part series on how to configure App Configuration and Key Vault to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).

  • [1/4] Injecting environment variables from App Configuration and Key Vault into AKS containers
  • [2/4] Configuring App Configuration to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Services
  • [3/4] Configuring Key Vault to expose environment variables to Azure Kubernetes Services
  • [4/4] Configuring Azure Kubernetes Services to consume ConfigMap and Secret resources

Key Vault

Azure Key Vault is one of several key management solutions in Azure and helps solve the following problems: Secrets Management, Key Management and Certificate Management. –Microsoft

It is assumed that you’re already familiar with Key Vault in Azure and that you have already set up your own Key Vault resource.

Concepts

To establish a connection between a Key Vault and an AKS container, you need to configure the following resources:

  • Azure Key Vault (with some keys, secrets or certificates).
  • A SecretProviderClass — a Kubernetes resource (which requires installation) that describes instructions for how keys/secrets/certificates can be pulled from a Key Vault and stored as Secrets.
  • A Secret — a Kubernetes resource (which comes out of the box) that stores secrets in Kubernetes for use by other resources — it will be automatically generated by the ServiceProviderClass.
  • A deployment or pod to consume the Secret.

Installation

Connecting to a Key Vault is realised through an Add-on in the Azure Kubernetes service.

If you’re using Bicep to deploy your application, you can configure the Azure Key Vault Secrets Provider in Kubernetes by enabling it:

resource aks 'Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters@2021-07-01' = {
  name: 'aks'
  properties: {
    addonProfiles: {
      azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider: {
        enabled: true
      }
    }
  }
}

If you’re not using Bicep to deploy your application, you can follow the instructions to use the Azure Key Vault provider for Secrets Store CSI Driver in an Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster.

You will also need the name of Azure Key Vault Service Principal GUID later. To retrieve this:

  1. Go to your Azure Kubernetes services and select the cluster.
  2. From the “Overview” screen, click the “JSON view”.

You will then find the GUID under the following section — copy it down as you will need it for later:

{
  "properties": {
    "addonProfiles": {
      "azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider": {
        "identity": {
          "clientId": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Authentication

Once you have created the AKS cluster, you will need to permit it to access your Key vault using Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). If you’re using Bicep, this command will grant administrative permissions:

// AKS cluster (existing resource, or replace with your definition)
resource aks 'Microsoft.ContainerService/managedClusters@2021-07-01' existing = {
  name: 'web-aks'
}

// Key vault (existing resource, or replace with your definition)
resource kv 'Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults@2022-07-01' existing = {
  name: 'web-kv'
}

var secretsClientId = aks.properties.addonProfiles.azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider.identity.clientId
var roleDefinitionId = resourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', '00482a5a-887f-4fb3-b363-3b7fe8e74483')

// Permit the AKS cluster to access the Key vault as a Key Vault Administrator
resource rbac 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
  name: guid(subscription().id, kv.name, secretsClientId, roleDefinitionId)
  scope: kv
  properties: {
    roleDefinitionId: roleDefinitionId
    principalId: secretsClientId
    principalType: 'ServicePrincipal'
  }
}

You can learn about the built-in roles for Key Vault data plane options from the Microsoft documentation.

If you’re managing your infrastructure manually or just want to test the resource, you can follow these steps:

  1. Go to your Azure Key Vaults and select the vault.
  2. Select “Access Control (IAM)” from the left blade menu.
  3. Click the “Add role assignment” button.
  4. Under “Role”, select “Key Vault Administrator” and click “Next”.
  5. Under “Members”, add a “Service principal”.
  6. Search for “azurekeyvaultsecretsprovider-” and select the Service Principal which corresponds to your AKS cluster.
  7. Review and assign the permissions.

Configuration

Accessing keys, secrets and certificates from a vault requires an extra step by comparison to the App Configuration. This is because:

  • Secrets in the key vault must be stored in kebab-case while environment variables are accessed in CONSTANT_CASE, so their names must be translated.
  • The SecretProviderClass needs to know the type for each object.
# web-keyvault.yml

apiVersion: secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io/v1
kind: SecretProviderClass
metadata:
  name: web-keyvault
spec:
  provider: azure
  secretObjects:
    - secretName: web-keyvault
      type: Opaque
      data:
        - key: APP_ENV                  # see note 1 & 8
          objectName: app-env           # see note 2
        - key: NODE_ENV
          objectName: node-env
  parameters:
    usePodIdentity: "false"             # see note 3
    useVMManagedIdentity: "true"        # see note 3
    userAssignedIdentityID: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
                                        # see note 4
    keyvaultName: "keyvaultname"        # see note 5
    tenantId: "azureTenantId"           # see note 6
    objects: |                          # see note 7 & 8
      array:
        - |
          objectName: app-env
          objectType: secret
        - |
          objectName: node-env
          objectType: secret

Some important notes based on the comments above:

  1. The environment variable name for use in the Kubernetes container.
  2. The name of the secret in the vault.
  3. The values of usePodIdentity and useVMManagedIdentity may appear to contain boolean values, they must be defined as strings.
  4. For the clientId, use the value which you retrieved at the end of the installation instructions.
  5. Configure the keyvaultName to contain your Key Vault.
  6. Configure the tenantId to contain the tenant of the Key Vault.
  7. The objects property ends with a pipe | defining it as a multiline string — it then contains another YAML document. The array of values define how the information is retrieved from the Key Vault. You can retrieve key, secret and certificate objects by defining the source appropriately here.
  8. Every objectName defined in the data array must also have a corresponding record in the objects document.

Deployment

You can then deploy your SecretProviderClass resource using kubectl:

kubectl apply -f web-keyvault.yml

If you’re using Azure DevOps, you can take advantage of the Kubernetes@1 task:

- task: Kubernetes@1
  inputs:
    connectionType: "Kubernetes Service Connection"
    kubernetesServiceEndpoint: "Kubernetes Service Connection Name"
    command: "apply"
    arguments: "-f web-keyvault.yml"

Debugging the SecretProviderClass

Once your resource has been applied, you can check to see if your resource exists using:

kubectl describe secretproviderclass web-keyvault

You can also see a list of your SecretProviderClass resources in the Azure Portal:

  1. Go to your Azure Kubernetes services and select the cluster.
  2. Select “Custom resources” from the left blade menu.
  3. Under “secrets-store.csi.x-k8s.io”, select “SecretProviderClass”.

Debugging the Secret

You can debug the Secret by using the following command:

# This will not work (yet)
kubectl describe secret web-keyvault

You can also see a list of your Secret resources in the Azure Portal:

  1. Go to your Azure Kubernetes services and select the cluster.
  2. Select “Configuration” from the left blade menu.
  3. Ensure the “Secrets” tab has been selected.

At this point, you may be expecting to also be able to see the Secret generated based on the SecretProviderClass; however, the ConfigMap will only be created once a container is created to consume its values (more on this in part 4).

Now that you’ve set up Key Vault, continue reading:
Part 4 of 4: Configuring Azure Kubernetes Services containers to consume ConfigMap and Secret resources.
